title: Cheapest Flights Within K Stops
description: There are n cities connected by some number of flights. You are given an array flights where flights[i] = [fromi, toi, pricei] indicates that there is a flight from city fromi to city toi with cost pricei. You are also given three integers src, dst, and k. Return the cheapest price from src to dst with at most k stops. If there is no such route, return -1.
constraints: There will not be any multiple flights between two cities
thinking_questions: This is shortest path with a constraint on the number of hops. Dijkstra's doesn't directly handle the hop limit — what does?
Bellman-Ford relaxes all edges iteratively. How do you limit it to k+1 relaxations?
Why do you need a copy of the previous distances before updating?

hints.L1: K-stop constraint = at most k+1 edges. Bellman-Ford with exactly k+1 relaxation rounds. Use a copy of distances to avoid using more than one new edge per round.
hints.L2: Initialize dist = [inf]*n, dist[src]=0. For each of k+1 rounds: copy dist. For each edge (u,v,w): if dist_copy[u]+w < dist[v], update dist[v].

follow_up_questions: Why must you use a COPY of dist in the inner loop? Give an example of what goes wrong without it.
How does this relate to standard Bellman-Ford? What's the k-limited version essentially doing?
Could you use modified Dijkstra's with state (node, stops_used)? What's the complexity?
solution_notes: Bellman-Ford with k+1 rounds. Copy dist before each round to prevent same-round chaining. O(k·E).
